coredata.dc:title: Low dark current and improved detectivity of hybrid ultraviolet photodetector based on carbon-quantum-dots/zinc-oxide-nanorod composites
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Sang-Won
Choi, Kyung-Jae
Kang, Byoung-Ho
Lee, Jae-Sung
Kim, Sae-Wan
Kwon, Jin-Beom
Gopalan, Sai-Anand
Bae, Jin-Hyuk
Kim, Eung-Soo
Kwon, Dae-Hyuk
Kang, Shin-Won
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we fabricated an ultraviolet (UV) photodetector by blending a hybrid photoactive layer (HPL) that is composed of a hybrid structure containing Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Zinc Oxide Nanorods (ZnO NRs). To observe the effective photo-inducing abilities of CQDs and ZnO NRs, we analyzed the electrical properties of a UV photodetector using an HPL of CQDs/ZnO NRs. Under an illumination of 365 nm UV light with an intensity of 1 mW/cm2, the UV photodetector exhibited a high detectivity of 8.33 × 1012 Jones, which is higher than that of a UV photodetector using a HPL of blended poly-n-vinylcarbazole (PVK) and ZnO NRs. Experimental results show that an HPL of blended CQDs/ZnO NRs can induce efficient charge extraction from CQDs and ZnO NRs. In addition, CQDs act as charge controllers that enable hole-electron separation in the device upon UV illumination. These results indicate that synthesized CQDs can substitute for a charge transport polymer (i.e., PVK) and that a UV photodetector using CQDs can exhibit high detectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum Dots
ZnO nanorods
UV photodetector
Hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots.

Schematic of fabricated hybrid UV photodetector and FIB cross section of devices: (a) structure, (b) bandgap diagram, (c) FIB cross section of PVK/ZnO-NR device, (d) FIB cross section of CQDs/ZnO-NR device.
Characteristics of synthesized CQDs: (a) XRD patterns and TEM image (inset), (b) FT-IR spectra.
Characteristics of synthesized ZnO NRs: (a) TEM image of ZnO NRs, (b) XRD patterns for ZnO NRs.

UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra:(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnO NRs, CQDs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite, (b) PL spectra of ZnO NRs, CQDs (inset), and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite, (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of PVK and PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite, (d) PL spectra of PVK and PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite.
AFM image of HPL deposited on ITO substrate: (a) PVK/ZnO NRs, (b) CQDs/ZnO NRs.
J-V characteristics of a hybrid UV photodetector: (a) PVK/ZnO NRs, (inset: ZnO NRs only) (b) CQDs/ZnO NRs.
PL decay curves of ZnO NRs, CQDs, PVK, PVK/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs film.
Spectral response of CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetector as reverse bias increases.
Dynamic range of PVK/ZnO-NRs (inset) and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetector measured at – 2 V.
Transient photoresponse waveform of PVK/ZnO-NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetectors with bias of −2 V.
Performance of hybrid UV photodetector with different HPL under 365 nm UV light, 1 mW/cm2 at a bias of -2 V

head.ce:title: Low dark current and improved detectivity of hybrid ultraviolet photodetector based on carbon-quantum-dots/zinc-oxide-nanorod composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Computer and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, Kyungil University, 33 Buho-ri, Hayang-eup, Gyeongsan-si 712-701, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we fabricated an ultraviolet (UV) photodetector by blending a hybrid photoactive layer (HPL) that is composed of a hybrid structure containing Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and Zinc Oxide Nanorods (ZnO NRs). To observe the effective photo-inducing abilities of CQDs and ZnO NRs, we analyzed the electrical properties of a UV photodetector using an HPL of CQDs/ZnO NRs. Under an illumination of 365 nm UV light with an intensity of 1 mW/cm2, the UV photodetector exhibited a high detectivity of 8.33 × 1012 Jones, which is higher than that of a UV photodetector using a HPL of blended poly-n-vinylcarbazole (PVK) and ZnO NRs. Experimental results show that an HPL of blended CQDs/ZnO NRs can induce efficient charge extraction from CQDs and ZnO NRs. In addition, CQDs act as charge controllers that enable hole-electron separation in the device upon UV illumination. These results indicate that synthesized CQDs can substitute for a charge transport polymer (i.e., PVK) and that a UV photodetector using CQDs can exhibit high detectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: An ultraviolet (UV) photodetector has a wide range of applications including in environmental and biological research, sensing, detection, and the launching of missiles [1]. To date, most of the commercial photodetectors are fabricated by means of film growth from single-crystalline Si, SiC, GaN, and ZnO, which requires vacuum processing [2–5]. Recently, UV photodetectors based on nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots, carbon materials, and gold nanoparticles (NPs) have become particularly attractive because of their potential applications in next-generation optoelectronic devices [6–8]. As a wide-band-gap semiconductor material, ZnO has drawn considerable attention because of its unique optical and electrical properties, one of which is a wide band gap (3.37 eV) [9–11]. In particular, various ZnO nanomaterials have been used to fabricate UV photodetectors with high photoconductive gain and high responsivity [12,13]. Unfortunately, these UV photodetectors suffer from poor transient response, which has been attributed to surface defects such as the grain boundaries of ZnO nanomaterials [14]. Thus, recent research on UV photodetectors based on hybrid material with carbon material-ZnO-NP composites has attracted much attention because of their efficient charge extraction. This has been attributed to the collection efficiency of the ZnO-based UV photodetector, a quality that contributes to high responsivity and a fast transient response.

The incorporation of carbon materials has received considerable attention because of the tunable surface functionalities, outstanding optical properties, thermal stability, and low toxicity of these materials. Carbon materials have been widely used in photo-electronics, biotechnology, and chemical and biological sensing [15,16]. Specifically, Carbon materials synthesize the bottom-up method such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Son et al. studied UV photodetectors using the wet spin-coating for ZnO NPs and a transfer method for the graphene sheet [17]. Current-voltage measurements of the UV photodetector at 300 K showed that the ratio of photocurrent to dark current was approximately 1.1 × 104. Shao et al. introduced a photodetector fabricated from a ZnO QDs-graphene composite and polymers. The photodetector demonstrated high sensitivity to UV light with a maximum photoresponsivity of 247 A/W at 325 nm [18]. The high photoresponsivity of this device was attributed to the high active surface to volume ratio of the ZnO QDs-graphene composite. Zhan et al. studied a new type of self-powered, visible-light photodetector fabricated from a thermal rGO-ZnO NPs hybrid nanostructure [19]. The distinct and fast photoresponse of this device was a direct result of enhanced charge transfer between rGO and ZnO NPs, as well as the excellent electric properties of rGO sheets.

Guo et al. fabricated UV photodetectors having lateral structures by employing ZnO- NPs/carbon-quantum-dots (CQDs) hybrid films and synthesized the CQDs using a top-down method [20]. The researchers found that CQDs can improve the surface defects of ZnO NPs and the carrier separation at the CQDs/ZnO-NPs interface. However, this UV photodetector has not been reported in a performance comparison of charge transport polymer/ZnO NPs with CQDs/ZnO NPs. In addition, it was fabricated using a lateral structure of a sapphire substrate. The lateral-structured UV photodetector using sapphire substrate was more expensive than the vertical-structured UV photodetector based on CQDs/ZnO NPs that have yet to be reported.

In this work, we analyzes the electrical properties to compare poly-n-vinylcarbazole (PVK)/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs-based UV photodetector. In addition, to fabricate a low-cost UV photodetector, we propose a vertical structure using CQDs/ZnO NRs. Our device simply consists of four layers excluding an electron-conducting layer, and can be produced by means of all solution processes. We find that the performance of a UV photodetector using CQDs can be improved to the carrier separation at the hybrid interface of CQDs/ZnO NRs. Thus, CQDs can be a good substitute for a charge transport polymer such as PVK and the detectivity of the UV photodetector can be improved.

The CQDs were synthesized using a previously proposed bottom-up method [21] that employs anhydrous citric acid (CA, Sigma-Aldrich) as the carbon precursor, octadecene (ODE, Sigma-Aldrich) as the noncoordinating solvent, and oleylamine (OA, Acros Organics) as the surface passivation agent, all of which are shown in Fig. 1 . A total of 15 ml of ODE and 2 ml of OA were loaded into a 50 ml three-neck flask, and degassed with argon for 30 min. When temperature was raised to 200 °C, 1 g of anhydrous CA was quickly injected into the reaction flask. After 20 min, the synthesized solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting dark-yellow solution was purified by being precipitated with ethanol and was subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min to obtain jelly-like CQDs. After being twice purified with ethanol, the CQDs were dried at room temperature. Finally, the CQDs were dissolved in chlorobenzene.

To synthesize ZnO NRs, we used an optimized sol-gel method [22,23]. A total of 11.5 mmol of zinc acetate dihydrate [Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, Sigma-Aldrich] was dissolved in 62.5 ml of anhydrous methanol and 2 ml of DI water was added to a 100 ml three-neck flask. The solution was heated to 60 °C by means of magnetic stirring. Then, 19.6 mmol of lithium hydroxide (LiOH, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to 32.5 ml of anhydrous methanol by gradual injection (1 ml/s). After 5 h, the mixed solution became turbid and ZnO NRs were grown. The synthesized ZnO NRs were then precipitated by centrifuging, washed repeatedly with anhydrous methanol twice, and finally dispersed in chlorobenzene, forming a translucent solution.

We fabricated a solution-processible UV photodetector as a vertical-structure device. The UV photodetector consisted of an anode (ITO), hole extraction layer (HEL), hybrid photoactive layer (HPL), and aluminum (Al) cathode. Fig. 2 provides schematic and energy band diagrams of the proposed UV photodetector. The energy levels of the CQDs can be found in Ref. [24]. And the thickness of each layer in these devices measured using a Focused ion beam (FIB) as shown in Fig. 2. A patterned glass substrate of 150 nm indium tin oxide (ITO), a typical anode material, was cleaned ultrasonically in acetone, methanol, and 2-propanol. An HEL of poly(ethylene-dioxythiophene):polystyrenesulphonate (PEDOT:PSS, Clevios™ PVP AI 4083, USA) was spin-coated on a pre-treated ITO-patterned glass substrate at 1500 rpm for 30 s, and annealed at 150 °C on a hot plate for 10 min, which gave a HEL film thickness of approximately 80 nm. To prepare the CQD/ZnO-NRs hybrid HPL, We blended the CQDs and ZnO-NRs solution in a ratio of 1:1 (by volume). The HPL was deposited on the PEDOT:PSS film using the spin-coating method at 1500 rpm for 30 s and was annealed at 110 °C for 30 min in a vacuum oven. The HPL had a thickness of approximately 370 nm. A 150 nm Al cathode was thermally deposited under high-vacuum conditions. The photoactive area was 9 mm2. In addition, the device based on the PVK/ZnO NRs was fabricated to compare it with a device that used CQD/ZnO NRs. The PVK/ZnO NRs had a thickness of approximately 400 nm. In our UV photodetector, all layers, excluding the electrodes were simply fabricated by spin coating.

To evaluate the device performance, we measured the current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics under a UV light source having a radiation power of 1.0 mW/cm2 and a 365 nm wavelength. The J-V data were measured using a computer-controlled voltmeter (Keithley 2400 series sourcemeter, Keithley Instruments Inc., USA) and LabVIEW software (National Instruments, USA). UV–Vis absorption was recorded by means of a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SHIMAZU UV-1700). The morphology of HPL was examined using an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Park Systems, Republic of Korea), and the transient response was measured with an oscilloscope (Tektronix), which was used to record the voltage variation in the resistor.

First, to analyze the crystallinity and size properties of CQDs, we obtained measurements using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 3 . The XRD pattern of Fig. 3 (a) shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.75°, which reveals the amorphous nature of the CQDs [25,26]. Fig. 3 (b) shows a TEM image of CQDs, which have particle sizes of 10 nm and a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation.

The surface states and composition of the CQDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to confirm the conjugate formation as shown in Fig. 3 (b). A broad absorption band of citric acid in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1 appeared in the hydroxyl groups (OH) and a band in the range of 1670–1820 cm−1 appeared in the carbonyl groups (CO).

CQDs absorbed stretching vibration CH at 2925–3069 cm−1, and the formation of amide groups was confirmed by the appearance of several typical peaks at 1583 cm−1(CN) and 1476 cm−1(NH). However, the CQDs exhibited almost no absorption of hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups. The results of the CQDs are consistent with the decreased oxygen content and the appearance of nitrogen [21]. The carbonyl group was converted into an amide group during the passivation process. Thus, we should be able to synthesize the CQDs by tuning the carbonization degree of CA.

We measured the current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of the CQDs to confirm the fundamental electrical properties, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The CQD device configuration was Al/CQDs/Al. The I-V curve of the CQDs was linear at bias voltage from −10 to 10 V and the resistance properties of CQDs measured as 0.5 kΩ. The I-V curve of CQDs were linear at this voltage range with a nearly constant differential conductance.

To verify the crystallinity and shape of the ZnO NRs, we analyzed them using TEM and XRD. The ZnO NRs exhibited a typical size-broadened reflection of wurtzite-structured ZnO NRs. From the TEM image, the size of observed ZnO NRs was 20 nm as shown in Fig. 5 (a). In Fig. 5 (b), the XRD results show that the diffraction peaks and relative intensities of the spectrum all correspond to Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards card no. 36-1451.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the absorption spectra of ZnO NRs, CQDs, and hybrid composite used in our research. We can see that the synthesized ZnO NRs have absorption peaks located near 358 nm. The absorption curves of the CQDs/ZnO NRs hybrid composite share the same line-shape as those of the ZnO NRs. By contrast, the CQDs possess the properties of broad-band absorption, as shown in Fig. 6(a). However, the visible regions of CQDs are negligible. Thus, UV photodetectors fabricated from the hybrid composite exhibited favorable performance. The PL spectra of the CQDs, ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid composites are shown in Fig. 6(b). The PL peaks of CQDs show a broad emission around 520 nm. Note that the aforementioned excitation dependent emission is a typical characteristic of CQDs [27–29]. The PL spectrum of ZnO NRs shows a dominant broad emission near 580 nm, which has been frequently observed in ZnO and can be attributed to the deep-level-related emission of ZnO [30,31]. The PL spectra of the CQDs and ZnO NRs mixed solution have the same lineshape (480–680 nm) as that of ZnO NRs and the emission intensity varies. This reveals that effective interactions between CQDs and ZnO NRs occur in the hybrid composite. In other words, because the absorption of the HPL is dominated by ZnO NRs, under UV light, electrons and holes will be generated mainly in the ZnO NRs, and some of the generated electrons can enter the CQDs or PVK [20]. Fig. 6(c) show that PVK and PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite has absorption peaks at approximately 348 nm. The absorption of the PVK/ZnO-NRs-hybrid composite increased compared to the PVK. The PL spectra of the PVK and PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite are shown in Fig. 6(d). The PL spectrum of PVK show an emission near 414 nm and the PL spectrum of PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite show both PVK and ZnO NRs properties. However, the PL spectrum of PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid composite shows the phenomenon of a red shift to a 375 nm wavelength because the absorption of ZnO NRs begin at the 380 nm wavelength.

We analyzed the root mean square (RMS) roughness of HPL using the AFM to confirm the aggregation phenomenon in ZnO NRs. As shown in Fig. 7 , the RMS roughness of the PVK/ZnO NRs HPL on the ITO substrate was 78.18 nm, and the RMS roughness of the CQDs/ZnO NRs HPL on the ITO substrate was 3.12 nm. The roughness of the PVK/ZnO NRs HPL increased as a result of the aggregation of ZnO NRs. When the ZnO NRs in the PVK matrix are doped, large clustered islands were formed by the strong inter-molecular electrostatic interaction of ZnO NRs. By contrast, the CQDs/ZnO NRs HPL showed the properties of reduced roughness. These results reveal that the blending of CQDs and ZnO NRs improved the coverage of the HPL on the ITO substrate. A plausible reason is that the inter-molecular interaction between the ZnO NRs and the oleylamine as the surface passivation agent of CQDs prevent the aggregation of ZnO NRs and consequently improves the packing of HPL. Under UV light, electrons and holes in the HPL will be generated mainly in the ZnO QDs, and some of the generated electrons can enter the CQDs. Considering that carbon can serve as electron transportation media [20], the electrons that enter into the CQDs can be quickly collected by the Al. Thus, the CQDs can prevent the aggregation of ZnO NRs and can lead to efficient charge transport in the devices.

To compare the electrical properties of the poly-n-vinylcarbazole (PVK)/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs, we measured the J-V characteristics of the UV photodetector under 365 nm UV illumination of 1 mW/cm2, as shown in Fig. 8 . The device based on PVK/ZnO NRs having a reverse bias of −2 V exhibited a dark current of 0.17 mA/cm2 and a photocurrent of 45.76 mA/cm2. In addition, the device having a reverse bias of −2 V based on CQDs/ZnO NRs exhibited a dark current of 8.87 × 10−5 mA/cm2 and a photocurrent of 1.4 mA/cm2. The J-V characteristics of the UV photodetector indicate that relative photo and dark current decrements occurred on the CQDs/ZnO-NRs-based device compared to PVK/ZnO-NRs-based device. The CQDs can be applied as the charge trap in the HPL because oleylamine can act as the surface passivation agent of CQDs. In addition, because CQDs prevent the aggregation of ZnO NRs, the leakage current of the device can considerably decrease unlike that in the PVK/ZnO NRs. Thus, the J-V characteristics of CQDs/ZnO NRs device decrease the dark current and photo current. However, in our study, the efficiency ratio of photocurrent to dark current of the CQDs/ZnO- NRs device increased rather than the PVK/ZnO NRs. Under UV illumination, CQDs/ZnO-NRs device can provide an improved efficiency ratio because the CQDs can cause a decrease of charge recombination in the ZnO NRs through a charge trap of CQDs. Thus, we believe that CQDs will increase the efficiency of CQDs/ZnO NRs device performance under UV illumination.

In other words, these results indicate that CQDs can improve the efficiency of a UV photodetector by replacing PVK as a charge transport polymer. In addition, it can reduce the leakage current of the UV photodetector. The J-V characteristics of a ZnO-NRs-only device are displayed in the inset of Fig. 8. In our study, the ZnO-NRs-only device unchanged the J-V characteristics of both the dark and photo currents. Because this device used only ZnO NRs without PVK or CQDs as charge transport materials, the J-V characteristics showed identical dark and photo currents. In addition, a device without charge transport materials can cause a leakage current in the device because of interface mismatch of ZnO NRs.

The UV photodetector consisting of a CQDs/ZnO-NRs HPL was evaluated regarding the effect of CQDs on device performance. The performance was evaluated by using the projected detectivity (D∗). This allowed us to estimate the signal-to-noise ratio from the dark current, which has previously been determined to contribute considerably to noise [32,33]. The detectivity can be expressed in the following equation.
(1)
D* =
(
J
light
-
J
dark
/P
in
)
/
(
2 qJ
dark
)
1 / 2
where Jlight is the photocurrent under 365 nm UV light, and Jdark is the dark current. Pin is the incident light intensity, and q is the electron charge (1.6 × 1019 C). The value of the photocurrent density (Jph) was determined using the equation Jph = Jlight – Jdark.

Table 1 shows the performance of hybrid UV photodetectors that use PVK/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs under UV illumination. The Jph of the devices using PVK and CQDs are 6.18 × 1012 and 8.33 × 1012 Jones, respectively. When the HPL was applied to the CQDs, the Jph of the device increased further. Although CQDs/ZnO-NRs device decreased the dark and photocurrents compared to those of PVK/ZnO NRs, the responsivity properties can be effectively improved because of the charge control of CQDs that enable the hole-electron separation in the device upon UV illumination.

To confirm further these charge transport processes, the PL decay was measured using a 375 nm laser pulse as an excitation source. Fig. 9 shows PL decay of ZnO NRs, CQDs, PVK PVK/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs, which fit with a biexponential function with parameters of amplitude (A) and decay time constant (τ), (i.e., f(t) = A1/exp(-t/τ1) + A2exp(-t/τ2)) [34]. Using amplitude-weighted average lifetime (τave), the lifetime of ZnO NRs, CQDs, PVK, PVK/ZnO NRs and CQDs/ZnO NRs films were estimated to be 10.37, 7.24, 7.87, 7.4, and 3.4 ns, respectively. The lifetime of ZnO NRs, CQDs, and PVK is longer than that of the hybrid layer and we can observe that the PL lifetime for the CQDs/ZnO NRs is distinctively shorter than that of the PVK/ZnO NRs, which confirms that some of the charge has been transferred from ZnO NRs to the CQDs in the CQDs/ZnO NRs. The effective charge transfer from the ZnO NRs to CQDs facilitates the hole-electron separation. Thus, the performance of the devices can be improved because electrons and holes in the devices can induce more efficient charge transfer at the interface of CQDs/ZnO NRs rather than at that of PVK/ZnO NRs.

To demonstrate the spectral response of the CQDs/ZnO-NRs-hybrid UV photodetector, we measured its spectral response. As shown in Fig. 10 , when the applied reverse bias increased from 0 to −5 V, the spectral response considerably increased into the short-wavelength region. The photo-response covers a short-wavelength of 300–380 nm. In addition, a maximum photoresponsivity of 0.14 A/W 350 nm was observed under a bias of −5 V.

To demonstrate the dependence of the photocurrent on incident light intensity, the photocurrent density at −2 V was measured under different illumination intensities of UV LED light as shown in Fig. 11 . The PVK/ZnO-NRs device provided increased photocurrent values unlike in the CQDs/ZnO-NRs device according to incident light. Because the PVK/ZnO-NRs device showed a relatively high dark current as a result of leakage current in the device, we observed that the PVK/ZnO-NRs device had high photocurrent values under UV illumination. In addition, we observed that the photocurrent increased with irradiation intensity of UV illumination by 10–15 mW/cm2. When the intensity of the excitation light was increased, the photocurrent of device was increased because more electron–hole pairs were generated. This occurred until all ZnO NRs were filled to a certain intensity. However, when the UV light intensity was higher than 15 mW/cm2, the photocurrent was saturated. When the intensity was increased further, the additional electron–hole pairs increased the probability of recombination as soon as they were generated [35]. Therefore, the properties of hybrid UV photodetector distinguished UV light at different intensities and saturated the photocurrent of the device when UV light intensity was increased considerably.

Finally, the photo-response speed was characterized by a digitally controlled short light pulse. As shown in Fig. 12 , the transient photocurrent of the PVK/ZnO-NRs and CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetectors was measured under a bias of −2 V at a light intensity of 1 mW/cm2 from a 365 nm UV LED light source. The current rapidly increased under UV illumination and exponentially decreased without UV illumination. In addition, the UV photodetector exhibited a steady response with repetitive UV illumination. The transient time can be defined as the rise time (when the photo current changes from 10 to 90% of the peak output value) and the fall time (when the photo current decays from 90 to 10% of the peak output value) [36]. The results of the PVK/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetector show a rise time of 25.6 ms and a fall time of 18.8 ms. In addition, the results concerning the CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetector show a rise time of 21.6 ms and a fall time of 13 ms. The transient photoresponse of the hybrid UV photodetector in this study is attributed to an improved carrier transport process between CQDs and ZnO NRs. The rise in the photocurrent involves steps of response time such as photon absorption, electron-hole pair separation, and electron-hole diffusion. Among these steps, the time required for electrons and holes to reach saturation concentration may dominate a device's rise time. When incident light is turned off, the opposite carrier recombination process will determine the fall time [37]. Thus, the CQDs are sufficient substitutes for charge transport polymers and can improve the photoresponsiveness of the device.

We fabricated a hybrid UV photodetector based on CQDs/ZnO NRs. By comparing PVK and CQDs, we analyzed the electrical properties of the devices. The CQDs/ZnO-NRs hybrid UV photodetector exhibited a high detectivity of 8.33 × 1012 Jones, which was higher than that of the PVK/ZnO NRs hybrid UV photodetector. The CQDs act as a charge controller that enables hole-electron separation into the device under UV illumination. Thus, a reduced leakage current can be induced in the proposed device, and efficient charge transfer result from using the CQDs. This work offers considerable opportunities for CQDs, which can be used in designing and fabricating many other UV photodetectors. In addition, we believe that this device is a promising option for fabricating low-cost UV photodetectors because the processes are compatible with large areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (No. NRF2014R1A2A1A11050377).
